game_name: Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back

design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling levels with blaster and lightsaber combat, collecting Force Power items to progress through Empire locations
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Hostile environment navigation combined with enemy encounters; resource scarcity (health/weapons) versus enemy density
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody Luke Skywalker fighting through Imperial forces across recognizable Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back locations (caves, ice planets, speeders)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Punishing difficulty design typical of early 90s NES platformers; refuses to accommodate casual play, demands mastery through repetition and pattern memorization

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives per stage with game over penalty
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No difficulty selection documented; fixed brutal difficulty curve demands player adaptation through repeated attempts

progression.upgrade_system: Force Power items provide temporary abilities or health restoration; blaster vs lightsaber weapon switching
progression.unlock_mechanism: Level progression tied to defeating stage objectives and collecting Force Power items; hidden developer credits accessible via Force Power collection in first level
replayability_hooks: High difficulty encourages repeated playthroughs to master patterns
Hidden sound test (Left + Select at intro screen)
Hidden developer credits (destroy ice wall in level 1 caves, collect Force Power)

world_building_techniques: Level design references iconic Empire Strikes Back locations (ice caves, Hoth speeder sections, Bespin)
Character sprites and environment art tie to film aesthetic
Minimal explicit narrative; film knowledge assumed as prerequisite

faction_indicators: Empire forces (Stormtroopers, TIE Fighters) as hostile entities
Luke Skywalker as player avatar (Rebel hero)
danger_signals: Enemy patrol patterns requiring evasion or combat
Limited health requiring careful resource management
environmental_storytelling: Hoth ice caves establish frozen planet setting
Speeder scene references planet-hopping narrative from film

sources_used: tcrf - Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor (description of ridiculously difficult side-scroller, developer credits mechanism, sound test, level design references)
